plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones who give us five great gifts — freedom from fear, the eye to see the truth, the path to walk, a refuge to shelter in, and awakening itself.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit abhaya (Skt. abhaya, 'non-fear', from a- + bhaya, 'fear') + daya (Skt. dāyaka / dāna, 'giver, bestower') + genitive-plural ending -āṇaṁ (Skt. -ānām).
wordByWord.notes: abhaya is the first and foundational gift — traditionally freedom from the seven worldly fears (sapta-bhaya); the daya-stem here means 'one who gives', not 'compassion'.

wordByWord.gloss: to the givers of sight (the eye of insight)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit cakkhu (Skt. cakṣu, 'eye, sight') + daya ('giver') + -āṇaṁ (gen. pl.). cakkhu < cakṣ, 'to see'.
wordByWord.notes: Read as the bhāva-cakṣu, the inner eye of right faith/insight (samyag-darśana), not merely physical sight.

wordByWord.gloss: to the givers of the path
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit magga (Skt. mārga, 'road, path'; doubled conjunct -gg- < -rg-) + daya ('giver') + -āṇaṁ (gen. pl.).
wordByWord.notes: magga is the path of liberation — in Jain terms the ratna-traya (right faith, knowledge, conduct).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saraṇa (Skt. śaraṇa, 'shelter, refuge', from śṛ/śri, 'to take recourse to') + daya ('giver') + -āṇaṁ (gen. pl.).
wordByWord.notes: Evokes the four refuges (cattāri saraṇāiṁ) — the Arihantas, Siddhas, Sādhus, and the Dharma.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bohi (Skt. bodhi, 'awakening, enlightenment', from budh, 'to awaken') + daya ('giver') + -āṇaṁ (gen. pl.).
wordByWord.notes: bohi here is the attainment of right insight/awakening; the culminating gift of the five.

literalTranslation: To the givers of fearlessness, to the givers of sight, to the givers of the path, to the givers of refuge, to the givers of enlightenment.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence to those who bestow fearlessness; who bestow the eye of true seeing; who bestow the path; who bestow refuge; who bestow awakening.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This single clause is a tight chain of five compounds, each ending in -dayāṇaṁ, 'to the givers of —'. The repeated daya-stem is the verse's whole point: the Arihanta does not merely possess these perfections, he gives them. The five are best read not as a flat list but as a ladder of one soul's rescue. First abhaya — fearlessness — because a mind gripped by fear cannot even begin; the teacher's mere presence and message quiet that terror. Then cakkhu, the eye — once steadied, the seeker is given sight, the inner vision (samyag-darśana) to perceive things as they are. Sight reveals a road, so next comes magga, the path to actually walk toward liberation. Along that hard road the traveller needs saraṇa, refuge — a shelter to lean on when strength fails. And the road's end is bohi, awakening itself, the very enlightenment the teacher embodies. The grammar reinforces the theology: five parallel datives of homage, anaphoric in -dayāṇaṁ, so the reciter feels the gifts accumulate, each presupposing the last. Where the preceding line hailed the Jina as the world's lamp and illuminator, this line turns that light into concrete benefaction — what the lamp's light actually hands to each soul that turns toward it.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The clause crystallises a core Jain conviction: the Tīrthaṅkara is upakārī, a benefactor, yet without violating the law that each soul must liberate itself. He 'gives' not by transferring merit but by teaching, exemplifying, and founding the path — the gift is opportunity and instruction, which the recipient must still actualise. The five map onto the soteriological sequence centred on bohi/samyaktva: cakkhu (right insight) and bohi (awakening) frame the doctrine of samyag-darśana as the gateway, while magga is the threefold jewel-path and saraṇa names the objects of refuge. abhaya, placed first, reflects the teaching that fearlessness is the seedbed of spiritual effort.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting these five, a practitioner can pause on each as a self-check: Have I let the teaching dissolve my fear? Am I using the eye of insight I was given? Am I actually on the path, not merely admiring it? Where do I take refuge under strain? Am I moving toward awakening? The clause turns praise into a personal inventory of the gifts received and used.

crossReferences.text: Aupapātika (Uvavāiya) Sūtra — Namutthuṇaṁ formula of homage to the Arihantas
Caturviṁśati-stava (Logassa) — the Jina as 'logassa ujjoyagare', light-maker of the world, paralleling the loga-/daya- titles
Doctrine of the four refuges (cattāri saraṇāiṁ) — arihanta, siddha, sāhu, dhamma

sources.title: Chatur-Vinshati Stava (Logassa Sutra), translation and notes — Y. K. Malaiya
sources.consultedFor: Section-by-section literal translation conventions for Āvaśyaka/Pratikramaṇa stava material and the Jina-as-illuminator imagery adjacent to this clause.

sources.title: Namotthunam Stotra — An English translation
sources.consultedFor: Cross-check of the line abhaya-/cakkhu-/magga-/saraṇa-/bohi-dayāṇaṁ rendered as granters of fearlessness, sight, path, refuge, and enlightenment.

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra (Namokar) Mantra — Pañca-Parameṣṭhi and the arihanta concept
sources.consultedFor: The doctrinal sense of arihanta as the worthy, fear-conquering, awakened teacher who is the object of homage in this stava.

notes: The five compounds are unambiguous and uniformly attested across Śvetāmbara translations. The daya-stem is glossed as 'giver' (Skt. dāyaka/dāna), not 'compassion' (dayā); this is the standard reading. cakkhu is interpreted as the inner eye of insight rather than literal eyesight, consistent with the spiritual register of the surrounding epithets. Some editions/manuscripts insert 'jīva-dayāṇaṁ' (givers of life) among these givers and order them slightly differently; the book's printed line here lists exactly five (abhaya, cakkhu, magga, saraṇa, bohi), which is followed. Verse is internally clause 5 of the Namutthuṇaṁ ('the five givers'); placement detail is left to the preface.
